quick_summary: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is one of the top three cloud providers, known for strengths in data analytics (BigQuery), Kubernetes (GKE), and machine learning (Vertex AI). It's essential for DevOps, data engineering, and ML engineering roles. Interview questions focus on service selection, architecture patterns, and cost optimization.

detailed_content.explanation:
Compute Engine provides virtual machines with machine types ranging from shared-core to high-memory and GPU-optimized instances. Preemptible/Spot VMs offer up to 91% discount for fault-tolerant workloads like batch processing and ML training. GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine) is the managed Kubernetes service — Autopilot mode eliminates node management entirely by letting Google handle the underlying infrastructure.

Think of Compute Engine as renting individual servers, while GKE is renting an entire data center orchestration layer. With GKE, you define what you want to run (pods, services, deployments), and Google handles placement, scaling, networking, and upgrades. Autopilot mode goes further — you don't even manage node pools; you just specify CPU/memory requests per pod.
detailed_content.example:
```bash
# Compute Engine: Create a VM instance
gcloud compute instances create web-server \
    --zone=us-central1-a \
    --machine-type=e2-medium \
    --image-family=ubuntu-2204-lts \
    --image-project=ubuntu-os-cloud \
    --boot-disk-size=20GB

# Spot VM for batch processing (91% cheaper)
gcloud compute instances create batch-worker \
    --zone=us-central1-a \
    --machine-type=n1-standard-4 \
    --preemptible

# GKE: Create an Autopilot cluster
gcloud container clusters create-auto my-cluster \
    --region=us-central1

# Deploy an application
gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-cluster --region=us-central1
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx:latest
kubectl expose deployment web --port=80 --type=LoadBalancer

# Check status
kubectl get pods
kubectl get services
```
detailed_content.key_takeaway: Use GKE Autopilot for production workloads (pay per pod resource requests, not per node) and Preemptible/Spot VMs for batch jobs — this combination can cut compute costs by 70-80%.
detailed_content.try_it: Create a GKE Autopilot cluster, deploy a simple Nginx deployment with 3 replicas, expose it via a LoadBalancer service, and verify all 3 pods are running with `kubectl get pods`.

pro_tip: In interviews, compare GKE Autopilot to AWS EKS and Azure AKS — mention that GKE's Autopilot is the most hands-off managed Kubernetes because it bills per pod, not per node. This shows multi-cloud awareness and deeper Kubernetes understanding.
